title: High-pressure magnetic transition in iron observed via diamond quantum sensing
authors: Kouki Yamamoto, Kenshin Uriu, Ryotaro Suda, Misaki Sasaki, Mari Einaga, Katsuya Shimizu, Kento Sasaki, Kensuke Kobayashi
abstract: Diamond quantum sensors offer high precision and spatial resolution as magnetic probes, making them promising for a wide range of applications. While diamond anvil cells (DACs) can generate extremely high pressures, techniques for magnetometry under such conditions remain limited. By fabricating an ensemble of NV centers directly on the anvil diamond surface, we enable precise magnetic measurements under high pressure. In this work, we employ this NV ensemble to image the stray magnetic field of iron up to 30 GPa, enabling the observation of the magnetic transition ($α$-$\varepsilon$ transition) in iron.
summary: This paper demonstrates a method for high-precision magnetic imaging under extreme pressure using NV centers on (111)-oriented diamond anvils. The technique successfully captured the ferromagnetic-to-paramagnetic transition in iron up to 30 GPa. This advancement provides a robust platform for studying magnetic properties of materials in high-pressure regimes.
main_problem: Performing precise magnetic imaging of small samples under ultrahigh pressure is difficult due to large background signals from the diamond anvil and signal degradation caused by lattice strain in standard (100)-oriented diamonds.
main_result: By using (111)-oriented diamond anvils with surface-fabricated NV centers, the researchers successfully imaged the magnetic transition (alpha-to-epsilon) in iron up to 30 GPa and observed pressure-induced hysteresis.
method: The team used Optically Detected Magnetic Resonance (ODMR) on an ensemble of NV centers fabricated via helium-ion implantation on the culet surface of a (110)-oriented diamond anvil cell.
model_or_system: The experimental platform is a Diamond Anvil Cell (DAC) containing an iron sample and NaCl pressure-transmitting medium, utilizing NV centers as quantum sensors to detect stray magnetic fields.
key_observables: Photoluminescence (PL) contrast, Zeeman splitting, Zero-Field Splitting (ZFS) shifts, and the spatial distribution of the stray magnetic field.
important_parameters: Pressure range up to 30 GPa, magnetic transition of iron (alpha-epsilon), and the gyromagnetic ratio of NV centers.
main_assumptions: The study assumes the use of (111)-oriented diamonds minimizes strain-induced signal degradation and that the pressure-transmitting medium (NaCl) remains sufficiently hydrostatic.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares NV orientations in (100) vs (111) diamonds and shows ODMR spectra; Figure 3 demonstrates magnetization reversal via external field reversal; Figure 4 shows spatial magnetic field maps during compression/decompression and a plot of magnetic field hysteresis.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the sensing platform and fabrication method, compares diamond orientations for strain resistance, details the experimental setup for high-pressure iron measurements, presents the observation of the magnetic phase transition and hysteresis, and concludes with future directions for ultrahigh pressure sensing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Schematic illustration of an NV center in a (100)-oriented diamond. Pink, blue, and semi- transparent gray spheres represent nitrogen, carbon, and a vacancy, respectively. The red arrow indicates the NV axis. In the (100) orientation, none of the NV axes are perpendicular to the culet surface, making them highly susceptible to strain under pressure. (b) Schematic illustration of an NV center in a (111)-oriented diamond. The NV axis shown is perpendicular to the culet surface, resulting in enhanced robustness against strain. (c) Representative ODMR spectra at 6.6 GPa with a bias magnetic field of 2.5 mT. The blue and red spectra correspond to the positions marked by blue and...

Figure 2. (a) Schematic illustration of the experimental setup. (b) Cross-sectional view of the sample cham- ber. NV centers are fabricated on the culet surface of the upper anvil diamond. The chamber is filled with NaCl, and both the iron sample and the ruby used for pressure calibration are embedded within the NaCl medium. (c) Optical microscope image of the sample chamber. The square-shaped iron sample is located at the center, and the ruby is enclosed by a red circle. (d) Microwave antenna. Microwaves are applied through the central opening, into which the DAC sample chamber is accommodated.

Figure 2(a) shows a schematic of the experimental setup. A 520-nm laser beam was reflected by a dichroic mirror and then focused onto the NV centers on the upper diamond anvil through an objective lens (Mitsutoyo, M-PLAN APO 50×, numerical

Figure 3. Magnetization reversal of the iron sample. The bias magnetic field has been subtracted. The dashed outline indicates the approximate position of the iron sample. The scale bar corresponds to 20 µm. (a) Stray magnetic field distribution in the initial state. The sample is magnetized to the right by applying an external magnetic field. (b) Stray magnetic field distribution after magnetizing the sample to the left with an external field. The distribution is reversed compared with (a). (c) Stray magnetic field distribution after magnetizing the sample to the right again. The distribution is similar to that in (a).

Figure 4. (a–g) Stray magnetic field distributions measured at different pressures. The bias magnetic field has been subtracted. The dashed outline indicates the approximate position of the iron sample. The scale bar corresponds to 20 µm. The disappearance of the stray magnetic field upon compression and its recovery upon decompression are clearly observed. (h) Hysteresis of the stray magnetic field from iron. The horizontal axis represents pressure, and the vertical axis shows the maximum value of the stray magnetic field. The compression and decompression processes are indicated by the red and blue curves, respectively.
